query: NESTROFT test uses osmotic fragility to screen for hereditary spherocytosis

retrieved_docs.title: Anemia Screening -- Diagnostic Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Osmotic fragility test: This test evaluates whether RBCs are more fragile than usual, which may indicate hereditary spherocytosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Immune Hemolytic Anemias -- Diagnostic Tests -- Osmotic Fragility Test
retrieved_docs.content: Osmotic fragility measures the ability of RBCs to withstand swelling and lysis in a hypotonic solution. Increased fragility is characteristic of certain types of hemolytic anemia, such as hereditary spherocytosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Anemia Screening -- Diagnostic Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic testing: This test can confirm conditions such as hereditary spherocytosis, pyruvate kinase deficiency, or other rare hereditary anemias.

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Spherocytosis  -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic basis revolves around history, clinical presentation, and laboratory results, which should include a blood smear, reticulocyte count, complete blood count, bilirubin level, and red cell osmotic fragility tests. Diagnosis is considered positive in patients who present Coombs-negative hemolysis with increased mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC), positive history for hereditary spherocytosis and spherocytes on microscopy, or by a positive result in a specialized test such as osmotic fragility, eosin-5-maleimide (EMA), or acidified glycerol lysis test (AGLT). [3] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Spherocytosis  -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Review the pathophysiology of hereditary spherocytosis. Describe the presentation of hereditary spherocytosis. Summarize the treatment of hereditary spherocytosis. Identify the importance of improving care coordination among the interprofessional team in the prompt recognition of hereditary spherocytosis and referring these patients to a hematologist for better management. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Spherocytosis  -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA) is a common cause of hemolysis, and it commonly presents in adult patients. Unlike elliptocytosis, however, patients with AIHA commonly test positive for Coombs, and this disease does not commonly correlate with a positive family history. [15] [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Spherocytosis  -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Most patients with hereditary spherocytosis have a positive family history. [8] This disease should be suspected in any child presenting with splenomegaly, anemia, and hyperbilirubinemia. Patients may also present with asymptomatic cholelithiasis of undetermined etiology, commonly with a positive family history. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Splenectomy -- Indications -- Congenital hemolytic anemias and hemoglobinopathies
retrieved_docs.content: Hereditary spherocytosis Hereditary elliptocytosis Pyruvate kinase deficiency Drepanocytosis or sickle cell disease [1] Thalassemia

retrieved_docs.title: Pyruvate Kinase Deficiency -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Laboratory evaluation is indicated for neonatal anemia or hyperbilirubinemia. Childhood chronic anemia with splenomegaly warrants investigation. Transfusion-dependent patients require evaluation. [4]  Complete blood count characterizes anemia. Hemoglobin levels vary for different severities of PK deficiency. A blood smear shows normochromic cells. Polychromasia with echinocytes may be seen. Spherocytes are absent. Macrocytosis indicates folate deficiency. [3] Reticulocytosis does not correlate with hemolysis. [9] LDH is elevated, and haptoglobin is low. Coombs' test and osmotic fragility are negative. Hemoglobin electrophoresis is normal.

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Spherocytosis  -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Hereditary spherocytosis has a broad severity spectrum, and patients will have different clinical courses. The degree of anemia categorizes as severe for patients with hemoglobin lower than 8 g/dL, moderate for 8 to 10 g/dL, or mild with hemoglobin 10 to 11.5 g/dL in females, or 10 to 13.5 g/dL in males. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Spherocytosis  -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: For patients who have moderate disease, the decision to perform splenectomy is controversial and extensive counseling, and educational plan are warranted before drawing a conclusion, there should be a consensus should between the patient, family, and physicians. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Spherocytosis  -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Hereditary spherocytosis, a type of congenital hemolytic anemia, is the most prevalent cause of hemolytic anemia due to an abnormal red cell membrane. These atypical erythrocytes are unable to maintain their normal biconcave shape due to genetic mutations in the membrane/cytoskeletal proteins that play a role in structural morphologic stability. The clinical manifestations vary according to the severity of the disease. This activity outlines the evaluation and management of hereditary spherocytosis and explains the role of the interprofessional team in providing quality care.

retrieved_docs.title: Anemia Screening -- Diagnostic Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase activity test: This test detects glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency, a common enzymatic cause of hemolytic anemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Anemia Screening -- Diagnostic Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Urinalysis: This test evaluates for the presence of hemoglobinuria or hemosiderinuria, which may indicate intravascular hemolysis.

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Spherocytosis  -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The overall prognosis for patients with hereditary spherocytosis is considered good. However, it may vary depending on the disease severity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2812
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia, which is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, is a hereditary hemoglobin disorder that can precipitate serious respiratory complications in adults. Clinically, داء الكريات المنجلية is associated with respiratory failure, respiratory insufficiency, and adult cardiac arrest, underscoring the need for vigilant respiratory monitoring and prompt intervention in patients affected by this disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2004
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Abnormal” is used as an interpretation of فقر دم فانكوني (Fanconi anemia), indicating that the clinical and laboratory findings associated with this genetic disorder are considered abnormal. Fanconi anemia is a hereditary condition characterized by bone‑marrow failure, congenital anomalies, and a heightened risk of malignancies, and the descriptor “Abnormal” reflects the pathological nature of its presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2441
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Abnormal” is used as an interpretation for both the Arabic phrase “البزل السّلوي غير الطبيعي” and the condition “فقر دم فانكوني.” In clinical practice, describing a spinal tap as “البزل السّلوي غير الطبيعي” indicates an abnormal cerebrospinal fluid result, while labeling “فقر دم فانكوني” as abnormal reflects the presence of Fanconi anemia, a hereditary bone‑marrow failure syndrome. Recognizing that “Abnormal” serves as a common descriptor for these distinct entities highlights its role as a generic marker of pathological or atypical findings in patient evaluation.

Laboratory evaluation, particularly measurement of total hemoglobin concentration, is interpreted against these interrelated conditions to guide diagnosis, risk stratification, and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1802
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and this hemoglobin disorder commonly manifests as نقص الأكسجين في الدم, indicating reduced oxygen levels in the blood.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2694
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 70, a high‑molecular‑weight plasma volume expander, is contraindicated in patients with الاعتلال الخثري (thrombotic disease). Because Dextran 70 can increase blood viscosity and promote coagulation, its use in individuals who already have a propensity for clot formation may exacerbate the underlying thrombotic condition and increase the risk of serious vascular complications. Clinicians should therefore avoid administering Dextran 70 to patients diagnosed with الاعتلال الخثري and consider alternative volume‑expanding therapies that do not carry this pro‑thrombotic risk.

Overall, the interrelationships among chronic kidney disease–related anemia, various deficiency and hemolytic anemias, thalassemia, and related immune‑mediated conditions underscore the importance of integrated diagnostic and therapeutic strategies that address both hematologic abnormalities and their associated systemic manifestations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2762
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and داء الكريات المنجلية is clinically associated with قساح.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2852
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 40, a low‑molecular‑weight plasma volume expander, is contraindicated in the presence of الاعتلال الخثري (thrombotic disease). Because Dextran 40 can increase blood viscosity and promote clot formation, its use in patients with thrombotic disorders may exacerbate the underlying condition and raise the risk of further vascular occlusion. Consequently, clinicians should avoid administering Dextran 40 to individuals diagnosed with الاعتلال الخثري and consider alternative volume‑expanding therapies that do not carry this risk.

Recognizing malaise, fatigue, and the link between immunity disorders and globulin deficiency, together with the shared pharmacologic strategies and contraindications, supports an integrated diagnostic and therapeutic approach in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2058
retrieved_docs.content: Human factor VIII is used as a therapeutic agent to treat coagulation and hemorrhagic disorders, helping to correct deficient clotting activity and control bleeding. By supplementing the missing or insufficient factor VIII, it restores normal hemostasis in conditions such as hemophilia A and other bleeding disorders, thereby reducing the risk of severe hemorrhage and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2353
retrieved_docs.content: Observation and evaluation of newborns for suspected condition not found is a specific form of medical examination/evaluation; it falls under the broader category of medical examinations and evaluations performed to assess health status. This particular assessment focuses on newborns when a suspected condition cannot be identified, ensuring that clinicians systematically observe and evaluate the infant to confirm the absence of disease or to detect subtle signs that may require further investigation. By classifying this newborn assessment as a subset of general medical examination/evaluation, healthcare providers recognize its role within routine pediatric care and its importance for early detection, reassurance, and appropriate follow‑up when a condition is not initially found.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2774
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, the Arabic term for sickle cell disease.
